---
title: "Sandbag Technique"
format: html
---

# 🛡️ Volunteer Guide: Proper Sandbagging Technique

[cite_start]During an emergency, speed is important, but technique is vital. [cite_start]A poorly built wall will fail when the water pressure rises. [cite_start]Follow these steps to ensure our sanctuary stays dry.

## 1. Preparation & Safety

* [cite_start]**Materials:** You will need burlap or plastic sandbags, coarse sand (avoid fine "play sand" if possible), shovels, and heavy-duty gloves.
* [cite_start]**The Two-Person Team:** One person holds the bag while the second person shovels.
* [cite_start]**Tip:** The person holding the bag should stand with their knees slightly bent to protect their back. [cite_start]Fold the top of the bag over to create a "handle" for easier filling.
* [cite_start]**Don't Overfill:** Fill bags only 1/2 to 2/3 full. [cite_start]This allows the bag to be "squished" into the shape of the bag below it, creating a tight seal.

---

## 2. Placement & The "Brick" Method

[cite_start]Never just throw bags into a pile. [cite_start]They must be laid with precision.

* [cite_start]**Clear the Area:** Remove all debris, ice, or large rocks from the area where the wall will sit.
* [cite_start]**The Staggered Fold:** Place the first bag with the "tucked" or tied end pointing upstream (the direction the water is coming from).
* [cite_start]**The "Brick" Pattern:** Lay bags like bricks in a wall. [cite_start]The middle of a bag in the second layer should sit directly over the "seam" where two bags meet in the first layer.
* [cite_start]**Tamp Down:** After laying each bag, step on it or hit it with a shovel to flatten it. [cite_start]This removes air pockets and forces the sand to fill the gaps.

---

## 3. The "Pyramid" Structure

[cite_start]If the water is expected to be more than one foot high, you cannot build a vertical wall; it will topple. [cite_start]You must build a Pyramid.

* [cite_start]**Rule of Thumb:** The base of your wall should be three times wider than the wall is high (e.g., If you need a 1-foot high wall, the base should be 3 feet wide).
* [cite_start]**Calking the Seams:** As you build upward, continue tamping the bags to ensure the layers "lock" together.

---

## 4. The Sealing Secret: Plastic Liners

[cite_start]Sandbags are porous—water will eventually seep through the fabric. [cite_start]To create a true barrier, use 6-mil plastic sheeting.

1.  [cite_start]Lay the plastic sheeting along the ground and up the wall/door you are protecting before placing the bags.
2.  [cite_start]Place the sandbags on top of the plastic on the ground.
3.  [cite_start]Lay the rest of the plastic over the outside (water side) of the sandbag wall.
4.  [cite_start]Weigh down the top edge of the plastic with another row of sandbags.

---

## 5. Cleanup & Disposal (Post-Flood)

[cite_start]**Warning:** Floodwater is often "blackwater," meaning it is contaminated with chemicals or sewage.

* [cite_start]**Wear Gloves:** Always wear heavy-duty gloves when removing bags that have been in contact with floodwater.
* [cite_start]**Do Not Reuse Sand:** If the bags were touched by floodwater, the sand is likely contaminated. [cite_start]Do not use it for playgrounds or sandboxes.
* [cite_start]**Safe Disposal:** Follow local municipal guidelines for disposing of "disaster debris".
